1: ḥallĕṣēnî yhwh mēʾādām rāʿ mēʾîš ḥămāsîm tiṣṣĕrēnî — "rescue me, O LORD, from evil men; protect me from violent men" — ḥālaṣ (to draw out, rescue, extricate — Piel) — originally to pull off a sandal, then extended to extracting from danger. ʾîš ḥămāsîm (men of violence, from ḥāmās, violence, wrongdoing) — ḥāmās is the pervasive OT word for physical and social violence that overturns right order; it is specifically what caused the Flood (Genesis 6:11,13). The superscription names David, suggesting the setting of Saul's court or the Absalom rebellion.
2: ʾăšer ḥāšĕbû rāʿôt bĕlēb kol-yôm yāgûrû milḥāmôt — "who scheme wickedness in their hearts and stir up conflicts every day" — ḥāšab (to plan, devise, calculate, reckon) — used for skilled craftsmanship (Exodus 31:4) and for deliberate scheming; the same mental intensity applied to both art and malice. kol-yôm (every day) — the constancy of their scheming makes it structural, not occasional. yāgûrû milḥāmôt (they stir up / assemble wars) — gûr in this context = to attack, stir up strife.
3: šānnĕnû lĕšônām kĕnaḥāš ḥămat ʿakšûb taḥat śĕpātêhem selāh — "they sharpen their tongues like a serpent; viper venom is under their lips" — šānan (to whet, sharpen) — the tongue as a blade being honed. The serpent (nāḥāš) metaphor links to the Eden narrative (Genesis 3:1-5). ḥămat ʿakšûb (the venom of a viper/asp) — ʿakšûb is a species of Palestinian viper. Paul strings this verse into his universal-sinfulness catena in Romans 3:13 ("the poison of asps is under their lips"), bracketed by Psalms 5:9 and Psalms 10:7 — the psalmist's description of particular enemies becomes, in Paul's reading, a diagnosis of universal human fallenness.
4: šomrēnî yhwh mîdê rāšāʿ miʾîš ḥămāsîm tiṣṣĕrēnî ʾăšer ḥāšĕbû lidĕḥôt pĕʿāmāy — "keep me, O LORD, from the clutches of the wicked; preserve me from violent men who plan to make me stumble" — yād rāšāʿ (the hand/power of the wicked) — yād as agency and power, not merely the anatomical hand. ḥāšĕbû lidĕḥôt pĕʿāmāy (they have planned to push aside/thrust my steps) — dāḥāh (to thrust, push away, throw down); pĕʿāmîm (steps, footfalls) — the wicked do not merely obstruct but actively plan to knock the psalmist off his feet.
5: ṭāmĕnû gēʾîm pah lî ûḥăbālîm pārĕśû rešet lĕyad-maʿgāl môqĕšîm šātû-lî selāh — "the arrogant have hidden a trap for me and stretched out cords; they have spread a net beside my path and set snares" — gēʾîm (the proud, arrogant ones) as the subject of concealed trapping — pride and cunning working together. pah (a spring-trap, snare), ḥăbālîm (cords, ropes), rešet (net), môqĕšîm (snares) — four different trap-instruments in a single verse, creating the picture of a path surrounded and booby-trapped at every point. The bird-and-hunter imagery connects to Psalms 124:7 and Psalms 141:9.
6: ʾāmartî layhwāh ʾēlî ʾattāh hăqîšîbāh yhwh qôl taḥănûnāy — "I said to the LORD, 'You are my God' — hear me, O LORD, listen to my pleas for mercy" — ʾēlî ʾattāh (you are my God) — the covenant confession is the foundation of the appeal. Amidst all the traps and violence, the psalmist's anchor is this relational identity: YHWH is my God, not merely a God. taḥănûnāy (my pleas for mercy, from ḥānan) — the petition follows from the relationship: because you are my God, hear my mercy-cry.
7: yhwh ʾădōnāy ʿōz yĕšûʿātî sakkōtāh lĕrōʾšî bĕyôm nāšeq — "O LORD the Lord, the strength of my salvation — you have shielded my head in the day of battle" — yhwh ʾădōnāy (the covenant name + the sovereignty name together) — this double divine address appears in several psalms and in Habakkuk 3:19, emphasizing both relational and sovereign dimensions. ʿōz yĕšûʿātî (the strength of my salvation) — YHWH as the source of saving power, not merely the One who saves. sakkōtāh lĕrōʾšî (you have covered/shielded my head) — sākak (to cover, screen, shield, as with a shield or canopy); the head as the most vulnerable target in battle.
8: ʾal-tittēn yhwh maʾăwiyyê rāšāʿ zimĕzāmô ʾal-tāpēq yārûmû selāh — "do not give the wicked what they want, O LORD; do not let their evil plans succeed, or they will grow proud" — maʾăwiyyê rāšāʿ (the desires of the wicked, from ʾāwāh, to desire, crave) — granting the wicked their desires would validate their methods. zimĕzāmô (their evil scheme/device, from zāmam, to plan, devise, usually with evil intent). yārûmû (let them be exalted, they would be lifted up) — the imprecation protects cosmic order: a wicked man whose schemes succeed becomes proud, threatening the justice structure.
9: rōʾš mĕsabbāy ʿămal śĕpātêmô yĕkhassĕmô — "let the trouble caused by the lips of those who hem me in fall back upon their own heads" — rōʾš mĕsabbāy (the head of those who surround me) — the leader or front person of the encirclement. ʿămal śĕpātêmô (the trouble/toil of their lips) — the verbal harm they have spoken. yĕkhassĕmô (let it cover them, engulf them, from kāsāh, to cover) — the words they have hurled at the psalmist should become a covering over themselves. The lex talionis logic: the trap set for another springs on the trapper.
10: yimmōṭû ʿălêhem gĕḥālîm bāʾēš yappilēm bĕmaḥămōrôt bal-yāqûmû — "let burning coals rain down on them; let them be thrown into the fire — into pits so deep they never rise" — gĕḥālîm (burning coals, glowing embers) — the same image as Psalms 120:4 (coals of broom wood). The imagery draws on the divine warrior arsenal (Psalms 18:12-13; Isaiah 30:30). maḥămōrôt (pits, depths, from ḥāmar, to be red/boiling?) — deep pits or slime pits (cf. Genesis 14:10 where the kings fell into asphalt pits). bal-yāqûmû (may they not rise) — the defeat is final.
11: ʾîš lāšôn bal-yikkôn bāʾāreṣ ʾîš-ḥāmās rāʿ yĕṣûdennû lĕmaddĕḥēpôt — "let no slander take root in the land; may evil relentlessly pursue the violent man until he falls" — ʾîš lāšôn (a man of tongue/slander) — the lying tongue that opened the psalm (v1-3) here receives its verdict. bal-yikkôn bāʾāreṣ (let him not be established in the earth) — kûn (to be established, set firm) — the slander-man should have no permanent footing. yĕṣûdennû lĕmaddĕḥēpôt (evil will hunt him to overthrows/downfalls) — dāḥāh (to push, thrust over) — the same verb as v4's plan to push aside the psalmist's steps.
12: yādaʿtî kî-yaʿăśeh yhwh dîn ʿānî mišpaṭ ʾebyônîm — "I know that the LORD will uphold the cause of the afflicted and defend the rights of the poor" — the psalm's confident affirmation after the imprecations: not wishing but knowing (yādaʿtî, perfect tense — settled knowledge). dîn ʿānî (the cause/case of the afflicted) and mišpaṭ ʾebyônîm (the justice/right of the poor/needy) — both legal-court terms; YHWH is the judge who takes up the case of those without human advocates.
13: ʾak ṣaddîqîm yôdû lišmĕkā yēšĕbû yĕšārîm ʾet-pānêkā — "surely the righteous will give thanks to your name; the upright will dwell in your presence" — ʾak (surely, only, truly) — the affirmative particle closing the psalm with confidence. yōdû lišmĕkā (they will give thanks/praise to your name) — yādāh (Hiphil, to praise, acknowledge) — the same root as tôdāh (thanksgiving). yēšĕbû ʾet-pānêkā (they will dwell in/with your presence) — the ultimate covenant blessing: dwelling with the face of YHWH (Psalms 17:15; Psalms 73:23-24).
